YouKn0wWho's blog

Editorial of Codeforces Round #752
By YouKn0wWho, 4 years ago,

Thanks for participating in my contest . I hope you liked the problems. I would love to hear your feedback in the comments.

If you find anything wrong in the editorial which is more likely to happen because I have written a rather long editorial to make you understand the solutions better, then comment below.

Also, don't forget to upvote to pay for the editorial. See you in my next contest!

1604A - Era

If ai>iai>i for some position ii, then we need to insert at least ai−iai−i new small elements before this position. Let m=max(0,maxi=1n(ai−i))m=max(0,maxi=1n(ai−i)). So we need at least mm operations. But its not hard to see that mm operations are enough. For example, you can insert mm 11s at the beginning of the sequence. This way, all elements will be shifted by mm positions, and consequently, will satisfy that ai≤iai≤i for each valid ii.

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
int main() {
  int t; cin >> t;
  while (t--) {
    int n; cin >> n;
    int ans = 0;
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
      int k; cin >> k;
      ans = max(ans, k - i);
    }
    cout << ans << '\n';
  }
  return 0;
}

1604B - XOR Specia-LIS-t

What happens if we split the sequence into subarrays of length 11?.

Yes, if nn is even, the bitwise XOR will be 00 as there will be an even number of 11s.

If nn is odd we can't do the same. But what if there is an index ii such that ai≥ai+1ai≥ai+1?

What if we use these two indices as a single subarray as it has LIS of length 11 and take other indices as single subarrays? Yeah, again, there will be an even number of 11s which will yield a bitwise XOR of 00.

What we are left with are strictly increasing sequences of odd lengths. Notice that any subarray of length ll has LIS of length ll here. So we need to find a sequence b1,b2,…,bkb1,b2,…,bk such that b1+b2+…+bk=nb1+b2+…+bk=n and b1⊕b2⊕…⊕bk=0b1⊕b2⊕…⊕bk=0 where nn is odd. Is it possible to find such a sequence? Pause and think.

Hint: think about the last bit of each bibi.

If XOR is 00, then there will be an even number of bibis such that its last bit is 11. But then the sum will be even. But here the sum nn is odd, which produces a contradiction.

So in the last case, it is not possible to find such a split.

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
int main() {
  ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
  cin.tie(0);
  int t; cin >> t;
  while (t--) {
    int n; cin >> n;
    vector<int> a(n + 1);
    bool inc = true;
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
      cin >> a[i];
      inc &= a[i] > a[i - 1];
    }
    if (n % 2 == 0 or !inc) {
      cout << "YES\n";
    }
    else {
      cout << "NO\n";
    }
  }
  return 0;
}

1603A - Di-visible Confusion

Notice that we can erase aiai at positions from 11 to ii. So for each ii, there should be at least one integer from 22 to i+1i+1 such that aiai is not divisible by that integer. If it is not satisfied for some integer ii, then there is no solution for sure. Otherwise, it turns out that a solution always exists.

Why?

We can prove it by induction. Let's say it is possible to erase the prefix containing n−1n−1 elements. As anan can be erased at some position from 11 to nn(let's say kk), then while erasing the prefix of n−1n−1 elements, when the prefix contains k−1k−1 elements, then anan is at the kk-th position, so we can erase it at that position and erase the rest of the sequence accordingly.

So we just have to check for all integers ii from 11 to nn, if aiai is not divisible by at least one integer from 22 to i+1i+1. Notice that if aiai is divisible by all integers from 22 to i+1i+1, then it means that aiai is divisible by LCM(2,3,…,(i+1))LCM⁡(2,3,…,(i+1)). But when i=22i=22, LCM(2,3,…,23)>109>aiLCM⁡(2,3,…,23)>109>ai. So for i≥22i≥22, there will always be an integer from 22 to i+1i+1 which doesn't divide aiai. So we don't have to check for them. For i<22i<22, use bruteforce.

Complexity: O(n+212)O(n+212).

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
int main() {
  ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
  cin.tie(0);
  int t; cin >> t;
  while (t--) {
    int n; cin >> n;
    bool ok = true;
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
      int x; cin >> x;
      bool found = false;
      for (int j = i + 1; j >= 2; j--) { // this loop will run not more than 22 times, in practice its much lower than that
        if (x % j) {
          found = true;
          break;
        }
      }
      ok &= found;
    }
    if (ok) {
      cout << "YES\n";
    }
    else {
      cout << "NO\n";
    }
  }
  return 0;
}

1603B - Moderate Modular Mode

If x>yx>y, then x+yx+y works, as (x+y)modx=ymodx=y(x+y)modx=ymodx=y and ymod(x+y)=yymod(x+y)=y.

The challenge arrives when x≤yx≤y. The later part of the editorial assumes that x≤yx≤y.

Claim 11: nn can't be less than xx.

Proof: Assume that for some n<xn<x, nmodx=ymodnnmodx=ymodn is satisfied. Then nmodx=nnmodx=n but ymodn<nymodn<n. So nmodxnmodx can't be equal to ymodnymodn which is a contradiction.

Claim 22: nn can't be greater than yy.

Proof: Assume that for some n>yn>y, nmodx=ymodnnmodx=ymodn is satisfied. Then nmodx<xnmodx<x but ymodn=y≥xymodn=y≥x. So nmodxnmodx can't be equal to ymodnymodn which is a contradiction.

So nn should be in between xx and yy. But what is the exact value of nn? Let's solve this intuitively.

Consider a line on the XX axis. Imagine you are at position 00. You will start jumping from 00 to yy with a step of length xx. So there will be a position from where if you jump one more time it will exceed yy. This position is p=y−ymodxp=y−ymodx. From this position let's go to yy in exactly 22 steps! Notice that y−py−p is guaranteed to be even as xx and yy both are even. So we need to jump with a length of y−p2y−p2 and we will jump to the position t=p+y−p2t=p+y−p2. And voila! tt is our desired nn because tmodx=y−p2tmodx=y−p2 and ymodt=(y−p)−y−p2=y−p2ymodt=(y−p)−y−p2=y−p2. To be precise, n=t=y−ymodx2n=t=y−ymodx2.

Here is a cute illustration for you:

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
int main() {
  ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
  cin.tie(0);
  int t; cin >> t;
  while (t--) {
    int x, y; cin >> x >> y;
    if (x <= y) {
      cout << y - y % x / 2 << '\n';
    }
    else {
      cout << x + y << '\n';
    }
  }
  return 0;
}

1603C - Extreme Extension

Let's find out how to calculate the extreme value of an array aa of nn integers. It turns out that a greedy solution exists!

Consider the rightmost index ii such that ai>ai+1ai>ai+1. So we must split aiai into new (let's say kk) elements 1≤b1≤b2≤…≤bk≤ai+11≤b1≤b2≤…≤bk≤ai+1 such that b1+b2+…+bk=aib1+b2+…+bk=ai . Notice that k≥⌈aiai+1⌉k≥⌈aiai+1⌉ because bk≤ai+1bk≤ai+1. But it is always optimal to make b1b1 as large as possible. It is not hard to see that the smaller the kk, the bigger the b1b1 we can achieve. So let's set k=⌈aiai+1⌉k=⌈aiai+1⌉. Now, notice that b1≤⌊aik⌋b1≤⌊aik⌋. So let's set b1=⌊aik⌋=⌊ai⌈aiai+1⌉⌋b1=⌊aik⌋=⌊ai⌈aiai+1⌉⌋. So we have performed k−1=⌈aiai+1⌉−1k−1=⌈aiai+1⌉−1 operations and will solve the problem analogously for the previous indices after replacing aiai by [b1,b2,…,bk][b1,b2,…,bk].

To sum it all up, we can calculate the extreme value in the following procedure:

  • Iterator from i=n−1i=n−1 to 11.
  • Add ⌈aiai+1⌉−1⌈aiai+1⌉−1 to the answer.
  • Set ai=⌊ai⌈aiai+1⌉⌋ai=⌊ai⌈aiai+1⌉⌋.

Pretty elegant! Let's call it elegant procedure from now on.

So we can calculate the extreme value of an array of nn integers in O(n)O(n).

To solve it for all subarrays in O(n2)O(n2), we need to fix a prefix and solve each suffix of this prefix in a total of O(n)O(n) operations. We can do that easily because the procedure to calculate the extreme values starts from the end, so we can sum up the contributions on the run.

How to solve the problem faster?

Think dp.

Let dp(i,x)dp(i,x) be the count of subarrays a[i;j]a[i;j] such that i≤ji≤j and after the elegant procedure xx becomes the first element of the final version of that subarray.

We only care about the xxs for which dp(i,x)dp(i,x) is non-zero. How many different xx is possible?

Well, it can be up to 105105, right?

Wrong!

Let's go back to our elegant procedure once again. For the time being, let's say for all x=1x=1 to 105105, dp(i+1,x)dp(i+1,x) is non-zero. So for each xx, we will add dp(i+1,x)dp(i+1,x) to dp(i,⌊ai⌈aix⌉⌋)dp(i,⌊ai⌈aix⌉⌋).

But there can be at most 2m−−√2m distinct values in the sequence ⌊m1⌋,⌊m2⌋,…,⌊mm⌋⌊m1⌋,⌊m2⌋,…,⌊mm⌋. Check this for a proof.

So there can be O(105−−−√)O(105) distinct xxs for which dp(i,x)dp(i,x) is non-zero. So we can solve this dp in O(n⋅105−−−√)O(n⋅105). To optimize the space-complexity we can observe that we only need the dp values of i+1i+1. So we can use only two arrays to maintain everything. Check my solution for more clarity.

To get the final answer, we will use the contribution technique. To be precise, for each (i+1,x)(i+1,x) we will add i⋅dp(i+1,x)⋅(⌈aiai+1⌉−1)i⋅dp(i+1,x)⋅(⌈aiai+1⌉−1) to our answer and its not hard to see this. Here, i⋅dp(i+1,x)i⋅dp(i+1,x) is the number of arrays where the ii-th element will be set to xx in the elegant procedure and ⌈aiai+1⌉−1⌈aiai+1⌉−1 is the number of operations that will be performed for the same.

Overall time complexity will be O(n⋅105−−−√)O(n⋅105) and space complexity will be O(n)O(n).

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
const int N = 1e5 + 9, mod = 998244353;
vector<int> v[2];
int dp[2][N];
int a[N];
int32_t main() {
  ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
  cin.tie(0);
  int t; cin >> t;
  while (t--) {
    int n; cin >> n;
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
      cin >> a[i];
    }
    long long ans = 0;
    for (int i = n; i >= 1; i--) {
      int k = i & 1;
      v[k].push_back(a[i]);
      dp[k][a[i]] = 1;
      int last = a[i];
      for (auto x: v[k ^ 1]) {
        int y = dp[k ^ 1][x];
        int split = (a[i] + x - 1) / x;
        int st = a[i] / split;
        ans += 1LL * (split - 1) * y * i;
        dp[k][st] += y;
        if (last != st) {
          v[k].push_back(st), last = st;
        }
      }
      for (auto x: v[k ^ 1]) dp[k ^ 1][x] = 0;
      v[k ^ 1].clear();
      ans %= mod;
    }
    cout << ans << '\n';
    for (auto x: v[0]) dp[0][x] = 0;
    for (auto x: v[1]) dp[1][x] = 0;
    v[0].clear(); v[1].clear();
  }
  return 0;
}

1603D - Artistic Partition

For now, let c(l,r)c(l,r) denote the number of integer pairs (i,j)(i,j) such that l≤i<j≤rl≤i<j≤r (instead of i≤ji≤j) and gcd(i,j)≥lgcd⁡(i,j)≥l. So we can add nn to f(n,k)f(n,k) in the end.

We can construct a straightforward dp where f(n,k)=mini=1n(f(i−1,k−1)+c(i,n))f(n,k)=mini=1n(f(i−1,k−1)+c(i,n)). As a straightforward implementation of c(l,r)c(l,r) takes O(n2log2n)O(n2log2⁡n) time, the total complexity of finding f(n,k)f(n,k) will be O(n5log2n)O(n5log2⁡n) which is quite shameful.

Let's see how to do better.

Tiny Observation: c(x,2⋅x−1)=0c(x,2⋅x−1)=0. It's easy to see why it holds.

Cute Observation: f(n,k)=0f(n,k)=0 when k>log2nk>log2⁡n.

Proof: Let L=log2nL=log2⁡n. Following the tiny observation, we can split the numbers as [1,1],[2,3],[4,7],…,[2L−1,2L−1],[2L,n][1,1],[2,3],[4,7],…,[2L−1,2L−1],[2L,n] without spending a single penny.

Now we can solve f(n,k)f(n,k) in O(n4log22n)O(n4log22⁡n) which is still shameful.

So we just have to find f(n,k)f(n,k) for 1≤n≤1051≤n≤105 and 1≤k≤171≤k≤17.

Let's optimize the calculation for c(l,r)c(l,r) .

c(l,r)=∑i=lr∑j=i+1r[gcd(i,j)≥l]c(l,r)=∑i=lr∑j=i+1r[gcd(i,j)≥l]
=∑k=lr∑i=lr∑j=i+1r[gcd(i,j)=k]=∑k=lr∑i=lr∑j=i+1r[gcd(i,j)=k]
=∑k=lr∑i=l,k|ir∑j=i+1,k|jr[gcd(i,j)=k]=∑k=lr∑i=l,k|ir∑j=i+1,k|jr[gcd(i,j)=k]
=∑k=lr∑i=1⌊rk⌋∑j=i+1⌊rk⌋[gcd(i⋅k,j⋅k)=k]=∑k=lr∑i=1⌊rk⌋∑j=i+1⌊rk⌋[gcd(i⋅k,j⋅k)=k]
=∑k=lr∑i=1⌊rk⌋∑j=i+1⌊rk⌋[gcd(i,j)=1]=∑k=lr∑i=1⌊rk⌋∑j=i+1⌊rk⌋[gcd(i,j)=1]
=∑k=lr∑i=1⌊rk⌋ϕ(i)=∑k=lr∑i=1⌊rk⌋ϕ(i)
=∑k=lrp(⌊rk⌋)=∑k=lrp(⌊rk⌋)
Here, p(x)=∑i=1xϕ(i)p(x)=∑i=1xϕ(i). We can calculate p(x)p(x) for all x≤nx≤n in O(nlog2n)O(nlog2⁡n) and calculate c(l,r)c(l,r) in O(n)O(n). So currently we can solve our problem in O(n3)O(n3) which is still not to be proud of.

But notice that there can be at most 2m−−√2m distinct values in the sequence ⌊m1⌋,⌊m2⌋,…,⌊mm⌋⌊m1⌋,⌊m2⌋,…,⌊mm⌋. Check this for a proof. So we can calculate c(l,r)c(l,r) in O(n−−√)O(n) which improves our solution to O(n2n−−√)O(n2n).

But notice that as c(l,r)=∑k=lrp(⌊rk⌋)c(l,r)=∑k=lrp(⌊rk⌋) we can precalculate the suffix sums for each r=1r=1 to nn over all distinct values of ⌊rk⌋⌊rk⌋ and then calculate c(l,r)c(l,r) in O(1)O(1). This preprocessing will take O(nn−−√)O(nn) time and O(nn−−√)O(nn) memory. That means we can solve our problem O(n2+nn−−√)O(n2+nn) which is promising.

Critical Observation: c(l,r)c(l,r) satisfies quadrangle inequality, that is c(i,k)+c(j,l)≤c(i,l)+c(j,k)c(i,k)+c(j,l)≤c(i,l)+c(j,k) for i≤j≤k≤li≤j≤k≤l.

Proof: Let f(i,j,r)=∑k=ijp(⌊rk⌋)f(i,j,r)=∑k=ijp(⌊rk⌋). Here,

c(i,l)+c(j,k)=f(i,l,l)+f(j,k,k)c(i,l)+c(j,k)=f(i,l,l)+f(j,k,k)
=(f(i,j−1,l)+f(j,l,l))+(f(i,k,k)−f(i,j−1,k))=(f(i,j−1,l)+f(j,l,l))+(f(i,k,k)−f(i,j−1,k))
=f(i,j−1,l)+c(j,l)+c(i,k)−f(i,j−1,k)=f(i,j−1,l)+c(j,l)+c(i,k)−f(i,j−1,k)
=c(i,k)+c(j,l)+f(i,j−1,l)−f(i,j−1,k)=c(i,k)+c(j,l)+f(i,j−1,l)−f(i,j−1,k)
But clearly f(i,j−1,l)≥f(i,j−1,k)f(i,j−1,l)≥f(i,j−1,k) as l≥kl≥k. So c(i,k)+c(j,l)≤c(i,l)+c(j,k)c(i,k)+c(j,l)≤c(i,l)+c(j,k).

You can learn more about quadrangle inequality and how it is useful from here. Read it because I won't describe why it helps us here.

This suggests that we can solve this problem using Divide and Conquer DP or 1D1D DP which will optimize our O(n2)O(n2) part to O(nlog22n)O(nlog22⁡n).

To solve for multiple queries we can just precalculate f(n,k)f(n,k) for 1≤n≤1051≤n≤105 and 1≤k≤171≤k≤17.

Overall complexity: O(nlog22n+nn−−√)O(nlog22⁡n+nn) where n=105n=105.

This problem can also be solved using Divide and Conquer DP and by calculating c(l,r)c(l,r) in O(r−l−−−−√)O(r−l) in each level which runs pretty fast in practice (for n=5⋅105n=5⋅105, it takes less than 3s) but I don't have a rigorous upper bound on the time complexity. Check out my solution for more clarity.

Code (1D1D DP)
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
const int N = 1e5 + 9;
const long long inf = 1e12;
using ll = long long;
int phi[N];
void totient() {
  for (int i = 1; i < N; i++) phi[i] = i;
  for (int i = 2; i < N; i++) {
    if (phi[i] == i) {
      for (int j = i; j < N; j += i) phi[j] -= phi[j] / i;
    }
  }
}
ll a[N], s1[N][320], s2[N][320];
int root[N];
ll c(int l, int r) {
  if (l > r) return inf;
  ll ans = 0;
  if (r / l <= root[r]) {
    return s1[r][r / l] - a[r / l] * (l - 1 - r / ((r / l) + 1));
  }
  else {
    return s2[r][l];
  }
  return ans;
}
ll dp[N][17];
int32_t main() {
  ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
  cin.tie(0);
  totient();
  for (int i = 1; i < N; i++) {
    a[i] = a[i - 1] + phi[i];
  }
  for (int i = 1; i < N; i++) {
    root[i] = 0;
    for (int j = 1; j * j <= i; j++) {
      s1[i][j] = s1[i][j - 1] + a[j] * (i / j - i / (j + 1));
      root[i] = j;
    }
    s2[i][i / (root[i] + 1) + 1] = s1[i][root[i]];
    for (int j = i / (root[i] + 1); j >= 1; j--) {
      s2[i][j] = s2[i][j + 1] + a[i / j];
    }
  }
  int n = 100000;
  dp[0][0] = 0;
  for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
    dp[i][0] = inf;
  }
  for (int k = 1; (1 << k) <= n; k++) {
    dp[0][k] = 0;
    vector<pair<int, int> > v; // (start pos, best j)
    v.push_back(make_pair(0, 0));
    for (int x = 1; x <= n; x++) {
      int j = (--lower_bound(v.begin(), v.end(), make_pair(x + 1, 0)))->second;
      dp[x][k] = dp[j][k - 1] + c(j + 1, x);
      for (int i = (int)v.size() - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
        int y = v[i].first, oldj = v[i].second;
        if (y > x && dp[x][k - 1] + c(x + 1, y) < dp[oldj][k - 1] + c(oldj + 1, y)) v.pop_back();
        else {
          int l = y + 1, r = n + 1;
          while (l < r) {
            int mid = (l + r) / 2;
            if (dp[x][k - 1] + c(x + 1, mid) < dp[oldj][k - 1] + c(oldj + 1, mid)) r = mid;
            else l = mid + 1;
          }
          if (r != n + 1) v.push_back(make_pair(r, x));
          break;
        }
      }
      if (v.size() == 0) v.push_back(make_pair(0, x));
    }
  }
  int q; cin >> q;
  while (q--) {
    int n, k; cin >> n >> k;
    if (k >= 20 or (1 << k) > n) {
      cout << n << '\n';
    }
    else {
      cout << dp[n][k] << '\n';
    }
  }
  return 0;
}
Code (D&C DP)
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
const int N = 1e5 + 9;
const long long inf = 1e12;
using ll = long long;
int phi[N];
void totient() {
  for (int i = 1; i < N; i++) phi[i] = i;
  for (int i = 2; i < N; i++) {
    if (phi[i] == i) {
      for (int j = i; j < N; j += i) phi[j] -= phi[j] / i;
    }
  }
}
ll a[N];
ll c(int l, int r) {
  if (l > r) return inf;
  ll ans = 0;
  for (int i = l, last; i <= r; i = last + 1) {
    last = r / (r / i);
    int x = 0;
    if (i >= l) x = last - i + 1;
    else if (last >= l) x = last - l + 1;
    ans += a[r / i] * x;
  }
  return ans;
}
ll dp[N][17];
void yo(int i, int l, int r, int optl, int optr) {
  if(l > r) return;
  int mid = (l + r) / 2;
  dp[mid][i] = inf;
  int opt = optl;
  ll cost = c(min(mid, optr) + 1, mid);
  for(int k = min(mid, optr); k >= optl ; k--) {
    ll cc = dp[k][i - 1] + cost;
    if (cc <= dp[mid][i]) {
      dp[mid][i] = cc;
      opt = k;
    }
    if (k <= mid) {
      if (cost == inf) cost = a[mid / k];
      else cost += a[mid / k];
    }
  }
  yo(i, l, mid - 1, optl, opt);
  yo(i, mid + 1, r, opt, optr);
}
int32_t main() {
  ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
  cin.tie(0);
  totient();
  for (int i = 1; i < N; i++) {
    a[i] = a[i - 1] + phi[i];
  }
  int n = 100000;
  dp[0][0] = 0;
  for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
    dp[i][0] = inf;
  }
  for(int i = 1; i <= n; i++) dp[i][1] = 1LL * i * (i + 1) / 2;
  for(int i = 2; i <= 16; i++) yo(i, 1, n, 1, n);
  int q; cin >> q;
  while (q--) {
    int n, k; cin >> n >> k;
    if (k >= 20 or (1 << k) > n) {
      cout << n << '\n';
    }
    else {
      cout << dp[n][k] << '\n';
    }
  }
  return 0;
}

1603E - A Perfect Problem

Let's go deeper into the properties of perfect sequences.

Observation 11: If a sorted sequence a1,a2,…,ana1,a2,…,an is perfect then all its permutations are also perfect.

Actually, it's very easy to see that as the order doesn't matter. So we will work on the sorted version of the sequence.

Observation 22: The sorted sequence a1,a2,…,ana1,a2,…,an is perfect iff all its subarrays are good.

Proof: Let's fix the minmin and maxmax of a subsequence. As min⋅maxmin⋅max is fixed, the worst case happens when we insert as many elements as possible in this subsequence which are in between minmin and maxmax as we are comparing min⋅maxmin⋅max with the sum, and the worst case is a subarray of this sorted sequence!

Now we will go one step further.

Observation 33: The sorted sequence a1,a2,…,ana1,a2,…,an is perfect iff all its prefixes are good.

Proof: The condition essentially means that a1⋅ai≥a1+a2+…+aia1⋅ai≥a1+a2+…+ai for all 1≤i≤n1≤i≤n. Imagine that its true. Now we will prove that the sequence is perfect. Consider a subarray [i;j][i;j]. ai⋅aj≥a1⋅ajai⋅aj≥a1⋅aj ≥a1+a2+…+aj≥a1+a2+…+aj ≥ai+ai+1+…+aj≥ai+ai+1+…+aj. That means all subarrays are good. And following observation 22, it indicates that the sequence is perfect. And it is necessary that all the prefixes should be good because they are subarrays after all which proves the iff condition.

Now let's exploit the fact that all aiai are ≤n+1≤n+1.

Let's remind again that we are considering the sorted sequences for now.

Observation 44: ak≥kak≥k is required for any kk.

Proof: If ak<kak<k, a1+a2+…+ak≥a1+a1+…+a1≥a1⋅k>a1⋅aka1+a2+…+ak≥a1+a1+…+a1≥a1⋅k>a1⋅ak (as k>akk>ak) which violates the good condition.

Observation 55: If ak=kak=k, then all ai=kai=k for i≤ki≤k.

Proof: a1⋅ak=a1⋅k≥a1+a2+…+aka1⋅ak=a1⋅k≥a1+a2+…+ak. This means (a1−a1)+(a2−a1)+…+(ak−a1)≤0(a1−a1)+(a2−a1)+…+(ak−a1)≤0. But as all ai≥a1ai≥a1, this can only happen when all ai=a1ai=a1. But as ak=kak=k, consequently, all aiai should be kk.

So anan can be either nn or n+1n+1. If an=nan=n then according to observation 55 there is only one such sequence. From now on, let's consider an=n+1an=n+1.

So a1⋅(n+1)≥a1+a2+…ana1⋅(n+1)≥a1+a2+…an. But this formulation is a bit hard to work on. So now we will make a move which I would like to call a programmer move. That is, the equation is equivalent to (a1−a1)+(a2−a1)+…+(an−a1)≤a1(a1−a1)+(a2−a1)+…+(an−a1)≤a1.

Following observation 33, its necessary that a1⋅ai≥a1+a2+…+aia1⋅ai≥a1+a2+…+ai for all ii.

Observation 66: if an=n+1an=n+1 and ai≥i+1ai≥i+1, then the prefix a1,a2,…,aia1,a2,…,ai is automatically good if the whole sorted sequence a1,a2,…,ana1,a2,…,an is good.

Proof: If the whole sequence is good then, (a1−a1)+(a2−a1)+…+(an−a1)≤a1(a1−a1)+(a2−a1)+…+(an−a1)≤a1. Now, (a1−a1)+(a2−a1)+…+(ai−a1)≤(a1−a1)+(a2−a1)+…+(an−a1)≤a1(a1−a1)+(a2−a1)+…+(ai−a1)≤(a1−a1)+(a2−a1)+…+(an−a1)≤a1. So, a1+a2+…+ai≤a1⋅(i+1)≤a1⋅aia1+a2+…+ai≤a1⋅(i+1)≤a1⋅ai So the prefix is good!

Let's recap that ai≥iai≥i is required(observation 44), if ai≥i+1ai≥i+1, then a1,a2,…,aia1,a2,…,ai is automatically good (observation 66) and ai=iai=i is only possible when i=a1i=a1(observation 55).

So the necessary and sufficient conditions for the sorted sequence aa to be perfect assuming an=n+1an=n+1 are -

  • For i≤a1i≤a1, a1≤ai≤n+1a1≤ai≤n+1
  • For i>a1i>a1, i+1≤ai≤n+1i+1≤ai≤n+1
  • (a1−a1)+(a2−a1)+…+(an−a1)≤a1(a1−a1)+(a2−a1)+…+(an−a1)≤a1

Notice that we need the answer considering all such sequences, not only sorted sequences, but it is not hard to track that using basic combinatorics. So let's fix the smallest number a1a1 and subtract a1a1 from everything. So we need to count the number of sequences b1,b2,…,bnb1,b2,…,bn such that -

  1. 0≤bi≤n+1−a10≤bi≤n+1−a1
  2. b1+b2+…bn≤a1b1+b2+…bn≤a1
  3. There is at least 11 number ≥n+1−a1≥n+1−a1, at least 22 numbers ≥n−a1≥n−a1, ……, at least n−a1n−a1 numbers ≥2≥2 (this condition is basically the intuition of when i≤a1i≤a1, a1≤ai≤n+1a1≤ai≤n+1 and when i>a1i>a1, i+1≤ai≤n+1i+1≤ai≤n+1).

Let's formulate a dp in top-down manner. We will choose bibi going from n+1−a1n+1−a1 to 00. Let dp(i,sum,k)=dp⁡(i,sum,k)= number of sequences when we already chose ii elements, the total sum of the selected elements =sum=sum and we will select kk now. So if we choose cntcnt numbers of kk, then the transition is something like dp(i,sum,k)dp⁡(i,sum,k) += dp(i+cnt,sum+cnt⋅k,k−1)cnt!dp⁡(i+cnt,sum+cnt⋅k,k−1)cnt!. And at the base case, when i=ni=n, return n!n!. The factorial terms are easy to make sense of because we are considering all perfect sequences, not only sorted ones. Check my solution for more clarity.

This dp will take O(n4⋅log2n)O(n4⋅log2⁡n) for each a1a1 (log2nlog2⁡n term is for harmonic series sum as cnt≤a1kcnt≤a1k) yielding a total of O(n5⋅log2n)O(n5⋅log2⁡n).

Observation 77: If a1<n−2⋅n−−√a1<n−2⋅n, then no perfect sequences are possible.

Proof: Assume that a1<n−2⋅n−−√a1<n−2⋅n, or n−a1>2⋅n−−√n−a1>2⋅n, or n−a1>2⋅n−−√n−a1>2⋅n. But because of the condition 33, there will be at least at least 11 number ≥n−2⋅n−−√+2≥n−2⋅n+2, at least 22 numbers ≥n−2⋅n−−√+1≥n−2⋅n+1, ……, at least n−2⋅n−−√+1n−2⋅n+1 numbers ≥2≥2. But that clearly means that b1+b2+…bn>n+1>a1b1+b2+…bn>n+1>a1 which is a contradiction.

So we need to traverse O(n−−√)O(n) a1a1s which also means in the dp kk is also O(n−−√)O(n). So the total time complexity will be O(n3n−−√log2n)O(n3nlog2⁡n) or faster but with very low constant that it will work instantly!

Phew, such a long editorial. I need to take a break and explore the world sometimes...

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
const int N = 205;
int mod;
 
int power(long long n, long long k) {
  int ans = 1 % mod; n %= mod; if (n < 0) n += mod;
  while (k) {
    if (k & 1) ans = (long long) ans * n % mod;
    n = (long long) n * n % mod;
    k >>= 1;
  }
  return ans;
}
int dp[N][N][40], fac[N], ifac[N];
int vis[N][N][40];
int n, a1;
/*
number of solutions to the equation b1 + b2 + ... bn <= a1
s.t. 0<=bi<=n+1-a1 
and there is at least 1 number >= n + 1 - a1, 
at least 2 numbers >= (n - a1), ..., at least (n - a1) numbers >= 2
*/
int yo(int i, int sum, int k) { // k <= 2 * sqrt(n)
  if (i == n) return fac[n];
  if (k == 0) return 1LL * fac[n] * ifac[n - i] % mod;
  if (vis[i][sum][k] == a1) return dp[i][sum][k];
  vis[i][sum][k] = a1;
  int &ans = dp[i][sum][k];
  ans = 0;
  int r = (a1 - sum) / k;
  for (int cnt = r; cnt >= 0; cnt--) {
    if (k > 1 and i + cnt < n - a1 + 1 - k + 1) continue;
    ans += 1LL * yo(i + cnt, sum + cnt * k, k - 1) * ifac[cnt] % mod;
    ans %= mod;
  }
  return ans;
}
int32_t main() {
  ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
  cin.tie(0);
  cin >> n >> mod;
  fac[0] = 1;
  ifac[0] = 1;
  for (int i = 1; i < N; i++) {
    fac[i] = 1LL * fac[i - 1] * i % mod;
    ifac[i] = power(fac[i], mod - 2);
  }
  int ans = 0;
  int lim = 2 * sqrt(n) + 1;
  for (a1 = max(1, n - lim); a1 <= n; a1++) {
    ans += yo(0, 0, n + 1 - a1);
    ans %= mod;
  }
  ans %= mod;
  cout << ans << '\n';
  return 0;
}

1603F - October 18, 2017

Idea: [user:YouKn0wWho], [user:Alpha_Q], [user:antontrygubO_o]

Thanks to Anton for writing editorial for this problem.

When x=0x=0, we need to count the number of sequences (a1,a2,…,an)(a1,a2,…,an), such that the aiais are linearly independent. Clearly, for n>kn>k there are no such sequences, and for n≤kn≤k, the answer is (2k−1)⋅(2k−2)⋅…⋅(2k−2n−1)(2k−1)⋅(2k−2)⋅…⋅(2k−2n−1), as aiai can be any element not generated by the elements a1,a2,…,ai−1a1,a2,…,ai−1, and they generate exactly 2i−12i−1 elements.

Now, let's deal with the case x>0x>0. It's easy to see that the exact value of xx doesn't matter here, it's easy to construct a bijection between such sequences for this problem and such sequences for the case x=1x=1, by change of basis from the one which has xx as the first element to one which has 11 as the first element. So from now on x=1x=1.

We will show that the answer for given n,kn,k is

(2k)n⋅2k−1−(2k−1)n⋅(2k−1−1)⋅2k−2+(2k−2)n⋅(2k−1−1)(2k−2−1)⋅2k−3…(2k)n⋅2k−1−(2k−1)n⋅(2k−1−1)⋅2k−2+(2k−2)n⋅(2k−1−1)(2k−2−1)⋅2k−3…

More generally, it's

∑i=0k((−1)i⋅(2k−i)n⋅∏j=0i−1(2k−j−1−1)2k−i−1)∑i=0k((−1)i⋅(2k−i)n⋅∏j=0i−1(2k−j−1−1)2k−i−1)

This is easy to calculate in O(k+logn)O(k+log⁡n), by calculating prefix products Pi=∏i−1j=0(2k−j−1−1)Pi=∏j=0i−1(2k−j−1−1) in O(k)O(k), and finding 2n2n in O(logn)O(log⁡n) and its powers in O(k)O(k). Now, let's prove the formula.

It's possible to prove the formula by some inclusion-exclusion arguments, but here we will present a bit more combinatorial approach. Let's just look at (2k−i)n⋅∏i−1j=0(2k−j−1−1)2k−i−1(2k−i)n⋅∏j=0i−1(2k−j−1−1)2k−i−1 and find some combinatorial interpretation for it.

Let's consider a linear space of dimension tt (with 2t2t elements), containing 11. Consider all its subspaces of dimension t−1t−1. Firstly, how many of them are there? Exactly 2t−12t−1, each corresponding to the only nonzero element of the orthogonal complement of the subspace. It's easy to see that exactly 2t−1−12t−1−1 of them will contain 11, and other 2t−12t−1 won't.

Then, there is a natural representation of the number ∏i−1j=0(2k−j−1−1)2k−i−1∏j=0i−1(2k−j−1−1)2k−i−1 — it's the number of sequences of spaces (S0,S1,S2,…,Si−1,Si)(S0,S1,S2,…,Si−1,Si), where:

  • S0S0 is the whole space of our problem, of dimension kk,

  • For jj from 11 to i−1i−1, SjSj is a subspace of Sj−1Sj−1 of dimension k−jk−j, containing 11.

  • SiSi is a subspace of Si−1Si−1 of dimension k−ik−i, not containing 11.

Then, (2k−i)n⋅∏i−1j=0(2k−j−1−1)2k−i−1(2k−i)n⋅∏j=0i−1(2k−j−1−1)2k−i−1 is the number of sequences (S0,S1,S2,…,Si−1,Si,a)(S0,S1,S2,…,Si−1,Si,a), where sets SS are described as above, and all elements of aa lie in SiSi (as there are 2k−i2k−i ways to choose each of them).

This starts to resemble what we need... As in such structure, space generated by aa won't be able to contain 11, and it's exactly the kind of arrays we are interested in.

So, we want to show that the actual number of arrays from the statement is equal to the number of tuples (S0,S1,S2,…,Si−1,Si,a)(S0,S1,S2,…,Si−1,Si,a), where the tuple is counted with a plus sign for even ii and with a minus sign for odd ii.

It's enough to prove that each array aa will be counted exactly once this way (meaning that it will be in tuples taken with plus one more time than in tuples taken with a minus). Fine, let's consider an array aa such that subspace spanned by it doesn't contain 11 and look at the sequences of sets (S0,S1,S2,…,Si−1,Si)(S0,S1,S2,…,Si−1,Si) such that aa is contained in SiSi. If the subspace generated by aa is TT, we just need all spaces to contain TT. If size of TT is tt, there is a bijection between such sequences and sequences (S0,S1,S2,…,Si−1,Si)(S0,S1,S2,…,Si−1,Si), where

  • S0S0 is the space orthogonal to subspace TT, of dimension k−tk−t,

  • For jj from 11 to i−1i−1, SjSj is a subspace of Sj−1Sj−1 of dimension k−t−jk−t−j, containing 11.

  • SiSi is a subspace of Si−1Si−1 of dimension k−t−ik−t−i, not containing 11.

But the number of such sequences for a fixed ii is precisely ∏i−1j=0(2k−t−j−1−1)⋅2k−t−i−1∏j=0i−1(2k−t−j−1−1)⋅2k−t−i−1! So, we need to show that the sum of this (with correspondent signs) over ii from 00 to n−tn−t is 11. Let's replace n−tn−t by nn, we need to show that

∑i=0k((−1)i⋅2k−i−1⋅∏j=0i−1(2k−j−1−1))=1∑i=0k((−1)i⋅2k−i−1⋅∏j=0i−1(2k−j−1−1))=1

This is easy to prove by induction, as after moving 2k−12k−1 to the right side it's equivalent to

∑i=1k((−1)i⋅2k−i−1⋅∏j=0i−1(2k−j−1−1))=(1−2k−1)∑i=1k((−1)i⋅2k−i−1⋅∏j=0i−1(2k−j−1−1))=(1−2k−1)
, and we can cancel (1−2k−1)(1−2k−1) from both sides and get the same equation, but now for k−1k−1.

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
const int N = 1e7 + 9, mod = 998244353;
template <const int32_t MOD>
struct modint {
  int32_t value;
  modint() = default;
  modint(int32_t value_) : value(value_) {}
  inline modint<MOD> operator + (modint<MOD> other) const { int32_t c = this->value + other.value; return modint<MOD>(c >= MOD ? c - MOD : c); }
  inline modint<MOD> operator - (modint<MOD> other) const { int32_t c = this->value - other.value; return modint<MOD>(c <    0 ? c + MOD : c); }
  inline modint<MOD> operator * (modint<MOD> other) const { int32_t c = (int64_t)this->value * other.value % MOD; return modint<MOD>(c < 0 ? c + MOD : c); }
  inline modint<MOD> & operator += (modint<MOD> other) { this->value += other.value; if (this->value >= MOD) this->value -= MOD; return *this; }
  inline modint<MOD> & operator -= (modint<MOD> other) { this->value -= other.value; if (this->value <    0) this->value += MOD; return *this; }
  inline modint<MOD> & operator *= (modint<MOD> other) { this->value = (int64_t)this->value * other.value % MOD; if (this->value < 0) this->value += MOD; return *this; }
  inline modint<MOD> operator - () const { return modint<MOD>(this->value ? MOD - this->value : 0); }
  modint<MOD> pow(uint64_t k) const { modint<MOD> x = *this, y = 1; for (; k; k >>= 1) { if (k & 1) y *= x; x *= x; } return y; }
  modint<MOD> inv() const { return pow(MOD - 2); }  // MOD must be a prime
  inline modint<MOD> operator /  (modint<MOD> other) const { return *this *  other.inv(); }
  inline modint<MOD> operator /= (modint<MOD> other)       { return *this *= other.inv(); }
  inline bool operator == (modint<MOD> other) const { return value == other.value; }
  inline bool operator != (modint<MOD> other) const { return value != other.value; }
  inline bool operator < (modint<MOD> other) const { return value < other.value; }
  inline bool operator > (modint<MOD> other) const { return value > other.value; }
};
template <int32_t MOD> modint<MOD> operator * (int64_t value, modint<MOD> n) { return modint<MOD>(value) * n; }
template <int32_t MOD> modint<MOD> operator * (int32_t value, modint<MOD> n) { return modint<MOD>(value % MOD) * n; }
template <int32_t MOD> istream & operator >> (istream & in, modint<MOD> &n) { return in >> n.value; }
template <int32_t MOD> ostream & operator << (ostream & out, modint<MOD> n) { return out << n.value; }
 
using mint = modint<mod>;
 
mint pw[N];
mint genius(int n, int k) {
  if (k == 0) return 1;
  vector<mint> c(k, 0), d(k + 1, 0);
  d[k] = 1;
  for (int i = k - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
    c[i] = pw[i] * d[i + 1];
    d[i] = (pw[i] - 1) * d[i + 1];
  }
  mint ans = 0, pwn = mint(2).pow(n), cur = 1;
  for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
    ans += ((k - 1 - i) & 1 ? mod - 1 : 1) * c[i] * cur;
    cur *= pwn;
  }
  return ans;
}
mint f(int n, int k) {
  if (k == 0 or n > k) return 0;
  mint ans = 1;
  for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    ans *= pw[k] - pw[i];
  }
  return ans;
}
int32_t main() {
  ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
  cin.tie(0);
  pw[0] = 1;
  for (int i = 1; i < N; i++) {
    pw[i] = pw[i - 1] * 2;
  }
  int t; cin >> t;
  while (t--) {
    int n, k, x; cin >> n >> k >> x;
    if (x) {
      cout << genius(n, k) << '\n';
    }
    else {
      cout << f(n, k) << '\n';
    }
  }
  return 0;
}

      »
      Gassa
      4 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +26

      The process that worked for me to solve it was:

      • Do a brute force for x<yx<y case, see all the possible answers for a few hand-made tests.

      • Look at one of an ends of the answers sequence (upper end works).

      • Observe that the number is close to yy.

      • Think of the solution in the form n=y−εn=y−ε where εε is very small.

      • Then ymodn=ymod(y−ε)=εymodn=ymod(y−ε)=ε.

      • And nmodx=(y−ε)modx=ymodx−εnmodx=(y−ε)modx=ymodx−ε.

      • The two are equal, so 2⋅ε=ymodx2⋅ε=ymodx.

      So, yeah, some amount of observation was required, but it mostly followed from looking at the brute-forced list of all possible answers.

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        chinesedfan
        4 years ago, show (+5) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        chinesedfan
        4 years ago, hide # ^ |
        ← Rev. 2 →  
        +4

        I solved it by very straightforward solution. 133667090

        • suppose, n = ax + r, and y = bn + r
        • then y = abx + (b + 1)r

        • if y < x, then ab = 0, just return x + y

        • else, try to divide ab as two integers and check if the answer is valid

        I don't know why it works. Hope someone can proof it or hack it.

        → Reply
        • »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          Logic_zys
          4 years ago, show (+1) #
          »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          Logic_zys
          4 years ago, hide # ^ |
           
          +16

          It's correct but you need to carefully check whether the factorization leads to a valid answer. a=floor(y/x), b=1, r=(y-ax)/2 is a solution (since x, y are both even, r will be integer).

          → Reply

            That' it! I divided from 1, so always got the answer you mentioned. Thanks.

        • »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          DevilNero
          4 years ago, show (+2) #
          »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          DevilNero
          4 years ago, hide # ^ |
           
          +8

          In the 3rd statement returning (x+y) was just an observation or you calculated. Can you elaborate plz

          → Reply
          • »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            Gassa
            4 years ago, show (+1) #
            »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            Gassa
            4 years ago, hide # ^ |
             
            +9

            A view of it:

            • The expression nmodx=ymodnnmodx=ymodn is complex.

            • And nn can be up to 2⋅10182⋅1018 according to the statement.

            • But if nn was large enough, the formula will transform into just nmodx=ynmodx=y, which is simple.

            • So let us think about large enough nn.

            • When x>yx>y, the expression nmodx=ynmodx=y means we can use n=x⋅k+yn=x⋅k+y for any large enough integer kk.

            • Turns out k=1k=1 is fine.

            → Reply

        Hey Gassa, can you please explain why ymod (y-e) = e ?

        → Reply
        • »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          Gassa
          4 years ago, show #
          »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          Gassa
          4 years ago, hide # ^ |
          ← Rev. 2 →  
          0

          Try to imagine it with concrete numbers:
          Let y=1000000y=1000000 and ε=5ε=5. Then
          1000000mod9999951000000mod999995 is simply
          1000000−999995=51000000−999995=5.

          Generally, the equation
          ymod(y−ε)=y−(y−ε)ymod(y−ε)=y−(y−ε)
          holds for 0≤ε≤y/20≤ε≤y/2.

          → Reply

jvmusin

← Rev. 2 →  
+11

For 1603A - Di-visible Confusion we can also go from the end of an array and try to remove each item, and after each removal repeat the process. If you passed the whole array and didn't find an element to remove, the answer is NO. It can be shown that in the worst case you will pass each element no more than 21-22 times (you proved it in the editorial), so this solution is also possible.

The only sad thing about this solution is that you need to calculate an actual index of your element, and in order to do that you may need Fenwick tree.

Solution: 133626329

→ Reply

    I think we do not need a Fenwick tree in this solution: simply shift each element after the removed one. (The complexity is good since each element is shifted at most 21 times.)

Whoa, i've got a simple solution for div.1 C, though i don't know how to prove its correctness (so maybe it's wrong).

We use brute force set r from n to 1, and move l to the left, when minimal hasn't changed we skip it.

And it passed (right now)!!!

+35

I solved Div1F with a different formula: the answer is

∑k−1l=0((nn−l)2⋅∏li=1(2k−2i))∑l=0k−1((nn−l)2⋅∏i=1l(2k−2i))

where

(nk)2=(1−2n)⋅⋯⋅(1−2n−k+1)(1−2)⋅⋯⋅(1−2k)(nk)2=(1−2n)⋅⋯⋅(1−2n−k+1)(1−2)⋅⋯⋅(1−2k)

is a gaussian binomial coefficient (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaussian_binomial_coefficient).

→ Reply
»
feecIe6418
4 years ago, show (+1) #

feecIe6418
4 years ago, hide # |
← Rev. 3 →  
+8

I have an O(nlog3n)O(nlog3⁡n) solution for D1D which sadly I wasn't able to debug during contest because of wasting time on A:

Just use segtrees to optimize calculating f(n,K)f(n,K). Precalculate g(n,k)g(n,k) which is the number of lls such that gcd(n,l)=kgcd(n,l)=k, and mainting a segtree with range add and range minimum. When we encounter nn, we insert f(n−1,k−1)f(n−1,k−1) into segtree, and go over all nn's divisors dd and add [1,d][1,d] by g(n,d)g(n,d).

→ Reply

Wielomian

Wielomian
4 years ago, hide # |
 
+23

Alternative solution for Div1A/Div2C:

  • notice, that it is always beneficial to erase last element if possible — it won't ever affect any other number in the array and it have to be removed at some point. Why not now?

  • If you can't remove the last element, then it's beneficial to erase second-to-last element, if possible. The proof is by similar argument to the previous one.

  • ...

  • In other words -- find the first element in reversed array that we are capable of removing. Execute this simple rule in a loop until array is empty (output YES) or no more elements can be removed (output NO).

  • Convince yourself that each element will be checked only a handful of times -- it's pretty hard to select a number that will be divisible by many of the indices i+1i+1, ii, i−1i−1, ... . As the original editorial proved, it's at most 21 times.

  • Implement code using std::liststd::list and std::find_ifstd::find_if (notice that find_if breaks when it finds first occurence) -- 133627167. Total complexity: O(21n)O(21n)

→ Reply

hackerbhaiya
4 years ago, show (+1) #

hackerbhaiya
4 years ago, hide # |
← Rev. 4 →  
+1

There is another solution to problem C as well:

Start from the end, if the last number is not divisible by (i+1) then erase it as it will not affect other elements. Now, find all the indexes which satisfy the given divisibility condition and store them in a priority queue. It is always good to delete elements (if possible) from the right. Also, calculate the count of consecutive values which will divide the number and we don't need to check this for more than 12-13 times.

Now delete the first element of the priority queue and update the values of all the indexes greater than the current deleted element and you are sure that you will not need to traverse each element more than 12-13 times, so it's fine.

End when the priority queue becomes empty (as this tells that all the divisible elements are removed) and check if all the elements are deleted or not.

My code : https://codeforces.com/contest/1604/submission/133638614

→ Reply

    0

    A priority queue is an overkill. A simple thing as you said select greedily from the right and delete it, it will be looking O(n*n) in nature but you will be iterating and cross one element not more than 20-21 times(because we also remove the particular element). So asymptotic complexity will be (20*n). My code: https://codeforces.com/contest/1604/submission/133691492

Div2A - 0.005 * 9529 = 47.645 USD
Div2B - 0.006 * 6274 = 37.644 USD
Div2C/Div1A - 0.008 * (4012+967) = 39.832 USD
Div2D/Div1B - 0.01 * (2273+911) = 31.84 USD
Div2E/Div1C - 0.04 * (27+396) = 16.92 USD
Div2F/Div1D - 0.2 * (1+24) = 5 USD
Div1E - 2 * 8 = 16 USD

Total - 200.881 USD

FYI I will get 400 USD for this contest. So the donation amount is indeed half of this as I have estimated before the contest, although didn't think that it would be this accurate.

burak

burak

← Rev. 4 →  
+5

PS: I GOT FST!

For Div.2 C what I did was for every ii, finding the nearest jj such that j+1j+1 is not divisible by a[i]a[i] and pushing the index ii to mp[i−j]mp[i−j]. Here, mpmp is a map of vectors. mp[d]mp[d] eventually stored the indices that become erasable after dd elements from their left gets erased.

Obviously we need to start by erasing some element in mp[0]mp[0]. Here, starting with the rightest element xx is the optimal because minimum number of elements gets negatively affected from this operation. So we erase xx from mp[0]mp[0]. After erasing xx we have to check whether there are new erasable elements to the right of xx and such elements can only be in mp[1]mp[1]. Thus we check whether the rightest element of mp[1]mp[1] (let's call it yy) is bigger than xx. If not, we have to keep erasing from mp[0]mp[0]. If yes, it is a good idea to first erase yy along with the indices that get erasable after erasing yy (It is a recursive process). Only after that we can continue with erasing from mp[0]mp[0].

Eventually all the vectors should become empty in mpmp. If they are empty then the answer is "YES", otherwise the answer is "NO".

Here is some in-contest ugly code but it failed the system test :( 133669599

What the code does is there is a function solve(k, iterator) and k means the rightest element we have deleted before calling this function and the iterator basically finds the most recent non-empty vector waiting for their elements to get erased. Can anybody help me what is wrong with my solution?

→ Reply

WiwiHo
4 years ago, show (+6) #
»
WiwiHo
4 years ago, hide # |
 
+40

Here is a simpler solution to div1C:

Assume that we want to calculate the extreme value of a1,a2,…,aia1,a2,…,ai. Let t[j]=t[j]= how many numbers ajaj is divided into, v[j]=v[j]= the smallest number ajaj is divided into. We know t[j]=⌈ajv[j+1]⌉t[j]=⌈ajv[j+1]⌉, v[j]=⌊ajt[j]⌋v[j]=⌊ajt[j]⌋. It can be observed that the extreme value of aj,aj+1,…,aiaj,aj+1,…,ai is ∑ik=j(t[j]−1)∑k=ji(t[j]−1).

For ii from 11 to nn, calculate t[j]t[j] and v[j]v[j] for all j≤ij≤i. When we add a new element aiai at the back, we can update t[j]t[j] and v[j]v[j] from i−1i−1 to 11 by brute force, but we stop the procedure when t[j]t[j] is not changed. It seems to be an O(n2)O(n2) solution, but for a certain jj, t[j]t[j] can be up to only O(C−−√)O(C) different values, so we only update a value at most O(C−−√)O(C) times, the solution is in O(nC−−√)O(nC) time.

Code: 133689083

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Gassa
    4 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    Gassa
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0

    My solution (133668323) is similar to this.
    The worst test case for this I found locally is 100000 99999 99998 ... 3 2 1, where the inner loop body is executed ~35 million times. It is a bit more than 100000⋅100000−−−−−−√100000⋅100000, but still far from that amount doubled, which is the theoretical upper bound. I wonder if there is a more clever test to push the count further.

    → Reply
  • »
    »
    lbm364dl
    4 years ago, show (+2) #
    »
    »
    lbm364dl
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    It may be a dumb question but I don't see why it's ok to stop when t[j]t[j] is not changed.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      Gassa
      4 years ago, show (+1) #
      »
      »
      »
      Gassa
      4 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +11

      The values t[j]t[j] and v[j]v[j] together fully define what happens to the left of them.

      For example, when you separate 1010 into three parts, you know the best way to do it is 10=3+3+410=3+3+4. Going to the left of this position, all you need to know is that all the parts have to be ≤3≤3. So, if you have already calculated what is the optimal solution on the left for parts ≤3≤3, there is no need to do it again.

      It's like memoization but without recursive calls.

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        lbm364dl
        4 years ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        lbm364dl
        4 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        Sorry I didn't notice the part to the left is still added to the answer when we stop. I wrongly thought we weren't adding it again. I got it now, thank you.

I have an alternate solution for Div 2C that I personally find easier. I found a simple solution where I simply store an array of numbers to represent how many positions each value of a must be shifted to no longer be divisible by i+1, then I walk through the array and ensure that each value is less than the amount of numbers preceding it in the array. This works because it can be proved that as long as the sequence can be reduced, there is always an optimal move that does not negatively affect any of the other positions, so you do not need to worry about shifting a number from a non-divisible status to a divisible status. Apologies for any confusion as I am not too familiar on how to format comments and I am not too skilled at describing algorithms. My code is here: https://codeforces.com/contest/1603/submission/133704780

Very cool contest! I personally quite enjoyed the observation/logic side... I don't think the maths were too difficult either, although I couldn't figure out 2D for x<y. Thank you for the fun problems.

→ Reply
»

YouKn0wWho

Div1 F — The explanation has many off-by-one errors. In particular, the answer for X≠0X≠0 should actually be

∑k−1i=0(−1)i(2k−1−i)n∏i−1j=0(2k−j−1−1)⋅2k−i−1.∑i=0k−1(−1)i(2k−1−i)n∏j=0i−1(2k−j−1−1)⋅2k−i−1.
if (X) {
	mi ans = 0;
	F0R(i,K) {
		mi cur = pow(mi(2),N*(K-i-1));
		F0R(j,i) cur *= po2[K-j-1]-1;
		cur *= po2[K-i-1];
		ans += (i&1?-1:1)*cur;
	}
	return ans;
}

Shouldn't nn be kk here?

Let's replace n−tn−t by nn, ...

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Benq
    4 years ago, show (+3) #
    »
    »
    Benq
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 4 →  
    +37

    It's also worth noting that counting the number of length-NN sequences such that the resulting subspace has dimension kk for each k∈[0,K]k∈[0,K] is given by

    #(k)=∏k−1i=0(2K−2i)⋅([tN−k]∏ki=011−qit)#(k)=∏i=0k−1(2K−2i)⋅([tN−k]∏i=0k11−qit)

    This can be computed with the q-binomial theorem (with q=2q=2). After computing these, the answer will be

    ∑Kk=02K−2k2K−1⋅#(k).∑k=0K2K−2k2K−1⋅#(k).

    Code: 133694728

    I was not aware that evaluating

    ([tN−k]∏ki=011−qit)([tN−k]∏i=0k11−qit)

    was doable but it seems that rainboy managed to find this link during the contest, congrats!

    → Reply

      »
      »
      Elegia
      4 years ago, show (+2) #
      »
      »
      »
      Elegia
      4 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +26

      The formula can be obtained by disturbing the GF. Let F(t)=∏ki=011−qitF(t)=∏i=0k11−qit, then we have (1−t)F(t)=(1−qk+1t)F(qt)(1−t)F(t)=(1−qk+1t)F(qt), hence a recurrence.

      → Reply

        »
        »

        Benq
        4 years ago, show (+1) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        Benq
        4 years ago, hide # ^ |
        ← Rev. 4 →  
        +16

        Not totally sure which formula you are referring to. Could you elaborate on how (1−t)F(t)=(1−qk+1t)F(qt)(1−t)F(t)=(1−qk+1t)F(qt) allows us to determine the coefficients of F(t)F(t)?

        EDIT: Ok, if we define

        ci=[ti]∏n−1i=011−qit,ci=[ti]∏i=0n−111−qit,

        then

        ci−ci−1=qici−qn+i−1ci−1⟹ci=qn−1+i−1qi−1⋅ci−1,ci−ci−1=qici−qn+i−1ci−1⟹ci=qn−1+i−1qi−1⋅ci−1,

        from which the result follows. Thanks!

For 1603C - EXTREME EXTENSION - Extreme Extension, the space can be reduced to O(sqrt(M)) where M = 100,000, the maximum possible input value. This is using the observation that if we have a quotient x/k, then min(k, x/k) ≤ sqrt(x), so instead of a single vector of length M, we can use two vectors of length sqrt(M) (one of the values of k, one for the values of x/k).

The solution still takes O(N sqrt(M)) time.

Code: https://codeforces.com/contest/1603/submission/134607321

→ Reply

I have a doubt in regards to the editorial for Div1 C. Shouldn't b1=ai+1modaib1=ai+1modai? If we set b1b1 to what is mentioned in the editorial, won't the updated value for a1a1 be wrong for the array [10,3][10,3]? As far as I understand, the updated value for a1a1 should be 11 here, but according to the editorial it seems to be 22.

Can someone please clarify?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    YouKn0wWho
    4 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    YouKn0wWho
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    It should be 22, because 22 is greater and it is possible to achieve this using 33(the minimum) operations: [10,3]→[2,2,3,3,3][10,3]→[2,2,3,3,3]. In your case, it is [1,3,3,3,3][1,3,3,3,3] using 33 operations. But if we can achieve 22 why bother with 11 which is lower?

    → Reply

0

There is a much cleaner solve for div2C/div1a (my solve is 133995726). Basically you find the highest non-divisor of a_i under i + 1 and check if that is less than 2. If any of these are less than 2 then the answer is NO, otherwise it is YES.

The reason this works is because the only time we cannot remove a number is when there are not enough numbers before it to get it to a value where it is not divisible. Otherwise it is enough to just remove the rightmost number that can be currently removed, which will always exist.

→ Reply

+56

I have a question with the time complexity of Div1.E.

In the observation 7,the solution improved the time complexity from O(n5logn)O(n5log⁡n) to O(n3n−−√logn)O(n3nlog⁡n) by only enumerating aiai from n−2n−−√n−2n to nn. But we still have O(n2n−−√)O(n2n) states，and the complexity of transfering is ∑n−a1+1k=1a1k∑k=1n−a1+1a1k ，which is still O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n) . So I think the time complexity is O(n4logn)O(n4log⁡n) ，and I don't know why my understanding is wrong. Can someone help me sort this out?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    TQX
    4 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    TQX
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +6

    I'm sorry that I found the mistake in my understanding. In fact,the complexity of transfering which is O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n) is actually the sum of transfering dp(i,j,1),dp(i,j,2),…,dp(i,j,k)dp(i,j,1),dp(i,j,2),…,dp(i,j,k) ，so the whole time complexity is O(n2)×O(nlogn)×O(n−−√)=O(n3n−−√logn)O(n2)×O(nlog⁡n)×O(n)=O(n3nlog⁡n). Sorry for wasting your time.

    → Reply

Problem F is an amazing problem. I have two solutions not listed in the editorial or in the comments.

I will prove the following claim: let f(n,l)f(n,l) be the number of nn-tuples of vectors in Zk2Z2k such that the spanning set of these vectors has size 2l2l. Then

f(n,l)=∏i=0l−1(2k−2i)(2n−2i)2l−2if(n,l)=∏i=0l−1(2k−2i)(2n−2i)2l−2i

First proof, by recursion and bashing. First observe that

f(n,l)=2lf(n−1,l)+(2k−2l−1)f(n−1,l−1)f(n,l)=2lf(n−1,l)+(2k−2l−1)f(n−1,l−1)

This is true by considering whether the last case increases the spanning set or not.

Now we induct on n and $l$.

Base Case: n=ln=l. Clear.

Inductive Step: It suffices to show that

∏i=0l−1(2k−2i)(2n−2i)2l−2i−2l∏i=0l−1(2k−2i)(2n−1−2i)2l−2i∏i=0l−1(2k−2i)(2n−2i)2l−2i−2l∏i=0l−1(2k−2i)(2n−1−2i)2l−2i
=(2k−2l−1)∏i=0l−2(2k−2i)(2n−1−2i)2l−1−2i=(2k−2l−1)∏i=0l−2(2k−2i)(2n−1−2i)2l−1−2i

We first cancel out the

∏i=0l−1(2k−2l)∏i=0l−1(2k−2l)

on both sides. This is equivalent to showing

∏i=0l−1(2n−2i)2l−2i−2l∏i=0l−1(2n−1−2i)2l−2i=∏i=0l−2(2n−1−2i)2l−1−2i∏i=0l−1(2n−2i)2l−2i−2l∏i=0l−1(2n−1−2i)2l−2i=∏i=0l−2(2n−1−2i)2l−1−2i

Now we expand the left hand side to get

1∏i=0l−1(2l−2i)(∏i=0l−1(2n−2i)−∏i=0l−1(2n−2i+1))1∏i=0l−1(2l−2i)(∏i=0l−1(2n−2i)−∏i=0l−1(2n−2i+1))
=∏i=1l−1(2n−2i)∏i=0l−1(2l−2i)(2n−1−2n+2l)=∏i=1l−1(2n−2i)∏i=0l−1(2l−2i)(2n−1−2n+2l)
=∏i=1l−1(2n−2i)∏i=1l−1(2l−2i)=∏i=1l−1(2n−2i)∏i=1l−1(2l−2i)

Dividing both sides by

2l−12l−1

yields the desired result.

The motivation for the first proof is that the numbers have a nice form.

Second proof, via polynomial. We first count the number of

2l2l

-element spans (S such that there exist $x_1,\cdots,x_l$ such that SS contains all and only elements of the form v1x1⨁⋯⨁vlxlv1x1⨁⋯⨁vlxl for vi∈0,1∀1≤i≤lvi∈0,1∀1≤i≤l in Zk2Z2k.

On one hand, there are ∏i=0l−1(2k−2i)∏i=0l−1(2k−2i) ways to pick ll linearly independent elements. On the other hand, there are ∏i=0l−1(2l−2i)∏i=0l−1(2l−2i) ways to select the same span.

Now, fix a span. We use inclusion-exclusion to find the number of ways to pick nn vectors such that their span has size 2l2l. It is not hard to show that this is a polynomial of degree at most ll in 2n2n (i.e. the answer for nn is P(2n)P(2n) for some degP≤ldeg⁡P≤l). Furthermore, for n=0,1,⋯,l−1n=0,1,⋯,l−1, the answer is 0, which implies P(x)=c∏i=0l−1(x−2i)P(x)=c∏i=0l−1(x−2i). For n=ln=l the answer is ∏i=0l−1(2l−2i)∏i=0l−1(2l−2i), implying c=1c=1, as needed.

Now the answer is simply

12k−1∑l=0min{k,n}f(n,l)(2l−1)12k−1∑l=0min{k,n}f(n,l)(2l−1)

because the probability that an element is in a randomly selected span of 2l2l elements is 2l−12k−12l−12k−1

I tried to format this, but sometimes the dollar signs don't render (they render on the overleaf compiler) and I have to use double dollar signs.

→ Reply
»

If x = y, then just print x If x < y, then print (x + y) /2 If x > y, then print x + y

Hello,
    In Problem 1603B - Moderate Modular Mode for the second case (when x ≤ y), can't we simply take the average ?
Because if n the average of x and y, it will be the same distance from x to n and from n to y ...
Is there any Counter Example ??

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Flyed
    4 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    Flyed
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    This only works if n % x == n — x and y % n == y — n, which is not always true. Consider x = 4 and y = 30, for example. If we use your strategy, n = (4 + 30) / 2 = 17. But 17 % 4 = 1 not 13.

    → Reply
»
CharlieV
3 years ago, show #
»
CharlieV
3 years ago, hide # |
 
0

Div1 E Complexity seems to be actually O(n3n−−√)O(n3n) with a constant of π218π218.

By eliminating all unnecessary transits, the result will look something like this:

==∑mn,i,j,k,i0[j+ik≤mn][i0i≤j]∑mn,j,kmn−jkjk∑mn,j(mn−j)j∑k1k2∑mn,i,j,k,i0[j+ik≤mn][i0i≤j]=∑mn,j,kmn−jkjk=∑mn,j(mn−j)j∑k1k2

The former ∑(mn−j)j∑(mn−j)j sums up to O(n3n−−√)O(n3n) because mn∈[n−2n−−√,n]mn∈[n−2n,n] with a constant of about 1/31/3, and the latter ∑1k2∑1k2 sums up to less than ζ2=π26ζ2=π26. The total constant is therefore π218π218.

I tested it with the following code, in which Cnt=469601774 when n=400 and Cnt=1054688268 when n=500, which matches the result quite well (the actual constant is a little smaller because of other optimizations)

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
#define For(i,a,b) for(int i=a;i<=b;i++)
#define Rev(i,a,b) for(int i=a;i>=b;i--)
#define Fin(file) freopen(file,"r",stdin)
#define Fout(file) freopen(file,"w",stdout)
#define assume(expr) ((!!(expr))||(exit((fprintf(stderr,"Assumption Failed: %s on Line %d\n",#expr,__LINE__),-1)),false))
using namespace std;
typedef unsigned long long ull;
typedef __uint128_t L;
struct FastMod {
	ull b, m;
	FastMod(ull _b) : b(_b), m(ull((L(1) << 64) / b)) {}
	inline ull reduce(ull a) {
		ull q = (ull)((L(m) * a) >> 64);
		ull r = a - q * b; // can be proven that 0 <= r < 2*b
		return r >= b ? r - b : r;
	}
};
FastMod F(2);
const int N=505; typedef long long ll;
int n,mod,ans,f[N][N][N],C[N][N],Cnt;
signed main(){
    cin>>n>>mod; F=FastMod(mod);
    For(i,0,n) { C[i][0]=1; For(j,1,i) C[i][j]=F.reduce(C[i-1][j]+C[i-1][j-1]); }
    ans=2; int B=sqrt(n)+2; For(mn,n-2*B,n) if(n+1-mn<=mn){
        Rev(i,n-1,1){
            if(n-i<=mn){
                f[i][0][0]=C[n][i];
                if(i*(n+1-mn)<=mn&&f[i][0][0]) ans=F.reduce(ans+f[i][0][0]);
            }
            For(j,0,mn){
                for(int k=max(1,n-i+(j!=0)-mn);mn+k<=n&&j+i*k<=mn;k++) {
                    for(int i0=i+1;i0<=n&&(i0-i)*k<=j;i0++){
                        Cnt++;
                        f[i][j][k]=F.reduce(f[i][j][k]+1ll*f[i0][j-(i0-i)*k][k-1]*C[i0][i]);
                    }
                    if(j+i*(n+1-mn)<=mn&&f[i][j][k]) ans=F.reduce(ans+f[i][j][k]);
                    Cnt++;
                    f[i][j][k]=F.reduce(f[i][j][k]+f[i][j][k-1]);
                }
            }
        }
        For(i,1,n-1){
            For(j,0,mn){
                for(int k=1;mn+k<=n&&j+i*k<=mn;k++) f[i][j][k]=0;
            }
        }
    }
    cout<<ans<<'\n';
    cerr<<"Cnt="<<Cnt<<'\n';
    cerr<<"Time = "<<clock()<<" ms"<<endl;
    return 0;
}

// START TYPING IF YOU DON'T KNOW WHAT TO DO
→ Reply
